title: REST API Basics: Resources, HTTP Verbs, and Status Codes
excerpt: A practical beginner-friendly guide to REST APIs: what resources are, how GET/POST/PUT/DELETE work, and which HTTP status codes matter most.

value.title: What a REST API actually is

body.value:
A REST API is a way for systems to communicate over HTTP using clearly defined endpoints.

In practice, it lets one application ask another application for data or request that something be created, updated, or removed.

The most important idea is this: the API exposes resources, and clients interact with those resources using standard HTTP methods.

value.title: Key idea
value.text: Think of a REST API as a contract for working with business resources through HTTP.

body.value:
A resource is usually a business object such as a user, order, invoice, or product.

Endpoints should be named around those resources, not around actions.

  • /api/users
  • /api/orders
  • /api/products

This is more scalable and easier to understand than action-based naming.

• GET - read data
  • POST - create new data
  • PUT - replace existing data
  • PATCH - partially update existing data
  • DELETE - remove data

Even when the back-end is complex, the external API should keep this behavior predictable.

value.caption: Basic REST interaction
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client] -->|GET /api/orders| B[REST API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B -->|200 OK + JSON| A

body.value:
Status codes are part of the API contract. They tell the client what happened.

  • 200 OK - request succeeded
  • 201 Created - new resource created
  • 204 No Content - success with no response body
  • 400 Bad Request - invalid input
  • 401 Unauthorized - authentication required
  • 403 Forbidden - authenticated but not allowed
  • 404 Not Found - resource does not exist
  • 500 Internal Server Error - unexpected server failure

value.title: Practical tip
value.text: Use status codes consistently. Clients should not need to guess whether an operation succeeded.

body.value:
Most REST APIs exchange data as JSON because it is lightweight, readable, and supported almost everywhere.

A typical response might return a collection, a single resource, metadata, or validation errors.

Good APIs use plural nouns, stable URLs, and clear resource hierarchy.

  • /api/customers
  • /api/customers/15/orders

Avoid names like /getOrders or /createUser because HTTP verbs already describe the action.

value.title: Simple controller example in ASP.NET Core

value.caption: Minimal REST-style controller example

body.value:
A good REST API is built around resources, uses HTTP methods consistently, returns meaningful status codes, and keeps naming predictable.

Once these basics are clear, topics like authentication, versioning, pagination, and API security become much easier to understand.
